---
title: "21. Robustness and Sensitivity"
---

[![Open the lab in Colab](https://colab.research.google.com/assets/colab-badge.svg)](https://colab.research.google.com/github/davi-moreira/2026F_evidence_driven_research_purdue_HONR464/blob/main/notebooks/student/nb09_attack_the_analysis_student.ipynb){target="_blank"}

> **The research decision.** Before you look at a single result, decide the full set
> of robustness and sensitivity checks you will run on your headline estimate, and
> commit to reporting all of them. That order, list first and look second, is what
> separates a finding you can defend from a flattering number you went fishing for.

## Why this decision matters

> *"Everyone shows me the version of the analysis that worked. I assume that one
> exists. My real question is what happened to all the reasonable versions you could
> have run instead, and whether you looked at them before or after you saw this
> answer."*
> — a **journal reviewer**, opening the first serious read your work will get

The reviewer is not accusing you of cheating. She is naming a plain fact about
analysis. Almost every real estimate depends on choices you could have made
differently, and any single choice can flatter the result. If you report only the
version that worked, you have handed her a number with no way to tell a delicate
truth from a lucky slice. This chapter gives you the habit that answers her before
she has to ask.

## The concept

An **estimate** is not one number. It is one number that depends on several choices,
and a critic attacks the choices. So you attack them first. Two named attacks do
this work, and people mix them up constantly.

A **robustness check** is re-running the same finding under a different but equally
defensible choice, to see whether the answer stays. Example: you reported an average
strength, so you also compute it after dropping the one specimen that failed at the
grip, and confirm the story holds. A **sensitivity check**, which some fields call a
**sensitivity analysis**, is deliberately changing an assumption to see how far the
answer moves. Example: you assumed your load cell was calibrated, so you ask how much
your result would shift if it were off by two percent.

Both attacks aim at the same target, the **headline estimate**: the single number
that stands in for your whole finding. You attack it from four handles, and any
honest plan turns one or more of them. The **sample** is which data you keep. The
**measurement** is how you turn a concept into a number. The **specification** is the
bundle of modeling choices behind the estimate. The **metric** is the yardstick you
report.

Turning every handle at once and plotting the results gives a **specification curve**:
a picture of one estimate under many defensible choices side by side. If the whole
curve stays on one side of zero, your direction is robust. Its spread is your honest
range.

The danger has a name. **Specification searching**, whose everyday name is
**p-hacking**, is running many analyses and reporting only the one that gave the
result you wanted, without disclosing the search. Its opposite is **pre-listed
checks**: writing down every check you will run, and committing to report all of
them, before you see any result. Same code, same table, opposite integrity. The only
difference is the order of looking and deciding.

## A worked example

Your lab tests whether a new heat treatment raises the **yield strength** of a steel
alloy, the stress at which the metal begins to deform permanently. Your headline: the
treated bars yield about twelve percent higher than the untreated ones. Before you
defend that number, you attack it across the four handles.

- **Sample.** All bars, or all bars except the one that fractured inside the machine's
  grip, which is a legitimate mechanical exclusion, not a convenient one.
- **Measurement.** Yield read by the standard 0.2 percent offset method, or read at
  the upper yield point.
- **Specification.** Force divided by the nominal cross-section, or divided by each
  bar's actual machined area.
- **Metric.** The gain in megapascals, or the gain as a percent.

You pre-list all sixteen combinations, run them, and plot the specification curve.
Every combination comes back positive: the treatment helps no matter which defensible
choice you make. The magnitude ranges from about plus eight percent to plus fifteen
percent. So the honest headline is not "twelve percent." It is "higher yield in every
specification we ran, by roughly eight to fifteen percent." Then you run one more
attack, a **placebo test**: you split bars that all got the *same* treatment into fake
"A" and "B" groups and confirm the gap between them sits near zero. If identical bars
showed a gap, your measurement, not the treatment, would be manufacturing it.

## The Colab laboratory

This chapter's lab is notebook **nb09 — Share the research + attack the analysis**.
Open it in Colab with the badge above and work the cells alongside this chapter.
There you rerun one headline gap across a pre-listed grid and read its specification
curve, watch specification searching manufacture a "significant" result from data with
no real effect, and run a placebo test and a leave-one-out influence check on seeded
code so you feel the difference between a robust finding and a fragile one.

## Recommended AI prompts

Commit your own answer first, then delegate. Each prompt is a checkable job, not a
request for a verdict.

**Locate the standard checks.**

```text
Act as a methods assistant for a two-group strength comparison. Before any code,
name the standard families of robustness and sensitivity checks for this design and
the four things I can vary to stress the estimate. Cite a real methods reference you
are confident exists; if you are unsure a source is real, say so.
```
After running, verify: open the reference and confirm the checks and the source
exist. Counters *confident fabrication* (an invented method or citation arrives as
confidently as a real one).

**Delegate, with a list to verify.**

```text
Here is my pre-listed robustness grid for one estimate: I vary the sample (all bars
/ drop the grip failure), the measurement (0.2% offset / upper yield point), the
specification (nominal / actual area), and the metric (MPa / percent). Name up to
three additional, equally defensible specifications I did NOT list, and for each say
which direction you expect it to push the estimate.
```
After running, verify: check each suggestion against the four handles and add only
the ones you can actually run on your bars. Counters *illusion of completeness* (a
tidy grid that quietly omits the one choice that would break the claim).

**Red-team your range.**

```text
Here is my claim: "higher yield in every specification we ran, by roughly 8 to 15
percent." Act as a hostile reviewer of a materials study. Name the single worst way
this could be specification searching rather than robustness, and the one check that
would expose it. Do not rewrite the claim for me.
```
After running, verify: run the check it names against your own data; the flaw is real
only if your numbers confirm it. Counters *sycophantic agreement* (praise that
reviews your ego, not your evidence).

::: {.callout-important title="Do not delegate"}
Three calls stay yours. You decide **which checks you commit to before you look**,
**which flagged issues the data actually confirms**, and **the final claim, with its
range and its boundary, that you defend**. A reviewer, human or AI, proposes. You
verify against your bars, and the evidence decides.
:::

## An AI failure case

You paste your four-handle grid and ask an AI to write your robustness section. Back
comes a long, well-organized passage that runs the sample handle, the specification
handle, and the metric handle, and closes with "the twelve percent result is robust
across all standard specifications." It reads as thorough and complete. The trap is
that it never listed the **measurement** handle, the 0.2 percent offset versus upper
yield point, and that is precisely the choice that moves your estimate the most. The
section looks exhaustive while omitting the one check a reviewer would reach for
first.

You catch it because you pre-listed all four handles yourself before opening the tool.
Reading its section against your own list, you notice measurement is missing, run that
comparison, and watch the estimate slide toward the low end of your range. A passage
that looks complete is not the same as one that is complete. You check its list
against yours, not against how finished it sounds.

## Verification laboratory

Verify this chapter's key result, the specification curve, with **alternative code**
from the [verification guide](https://github.com/davi-moreira/2026F_evidence_driven_research_purdue_HONR464/blob/main/ai_resources/verification_guide.md):
recompute one point of the curve a second, simpler way and compare. Take a single
specification, filter the two groups by hand, subtract their mean strengths, and
confirm the number matches what the automated grid printed. Two roads to one answer is
a verified point. Then use a **counterexample** hunt on the whole curve: try to build
one defensible specification that flips the sign. If you cannot find one after an
honest search, your "direction is robust" claim has earned its place. If you can, that
specification belongs in your report, not your recycling bin.

## Project transfer

This chapter turns into your **M9 research audit and poster draft**. Take the estimate
you produced in M8 and write a short, pre-listed robustness plan for it: three checks,
in order, chosen for the choices a hostile reviewer is most likely to attack, plus one
placebo or leave-one-out check. Run all of them, report the whole picture, and record
each as a row in your AI Research Ledger with the verification method that earned your
trust. That pre-listed plan, and the range it produces, is the spine of what you
defend at Friday's studio.

## Defend your decision

In four sentences, defend your robustness plan. State your headline as a direction
plus a range, not a single number, and name the one check that would most likely have
flipped it. State the boundary: name one thing a passed robustness plan does **not**
establish, such as turning a correlation into a cause. Close with your uncertainty:
the one specification you still are not sure about, and the check you would run next
to settle it.
